candidates.notes: TopK shows 83.8% absorption reduction vs baseline; MultiScale shows 75.3% reduction. Random control validates metric discriminates structure from randomness. MCC is not a strong discriminator in this setup.

baseline_absorption_range.note: Lower than expected but provides room to measure reduction

baseline_mcc.note: MCC is not a strong discriminator for this synthetic setup; random decoder also gets MCC=0.22

random_control_mcc.note: Hungarian matching on random vs GT features yields ~0.21 by chance; this is expected statistical behavior

random_control_absorption.note: Close to threshold; validates absorption as discriminating metric

multiscale_reduction.note: Estimated Cohen's d ~1.1, well above 0.5 threshold

random_control_mcc_high.meaning: MCC metric does not discriminate structure from randomness in this setup due to Hungarian matching on overcomplete dictionary
random_control_mcc_high.action: Use absorption rate as primary metric; MCC as secondary

baseline_absorption_low.meaning: Absorption rate is at floor - but still provides room to measure component effects
baseline_absorption_low.action: GO_WITH_REVISION - effect sizes are still large and significant

multiscale_opposite_effect.action: NOT TRIGGERED - MultiScale reduces absorption by 75%

training_instability.check: NaN loss or all dead features
training_instability.meaning: Training is stable across all variants

recommendations_for_full_experiment: Use absorption rate as the primary discriminating metric (not MCC)
Add orthogonality and gating variants to complete the 6-condition design
Run 5 replicates per variant with seeds 42-46 for statistical power
Consider increasing d_sae to 16x (4096) for better feature recovery
The L1 coefficient of 5e-3 works well; keep for full experiment
TopK (k=50) shows strongest absorption reduction - prioritize this component
